data.body:
PASS 1 review — criterion T5c (Security overlay) — CLEAN, no findings.

T5c is an overlay that applies ONLY where the plan adds a real security surface in this application's domain. This close-out ticket adds none: its scope is (a) emptying a ruff `per-file-ignores` allowlist, (b) re-validating `# noqa: BLE001` / `# noqa: T201` suppression reasons and deleting dead annotations, (c) documenting the error-handling convention in `CLAUDE.md` / a contributing doc, and (d) a final `make lint` + test + joe-janitor audit gate. No new endpoint/path, no network exposure, no authn/authz boundary, no storage/transmission of sensitive data or PII, and no credential/secret/grant is introduced or modified.

AFFIRMATIONS:
- (a) Auth on sensitive paths — N/A: no endpoint/path with an access level is added; this is a local, git-backed CLI/MCP tool's internal lint hygiene work.
- (b) Encryption at rest/in transit; no secret-logging/data-leakage — N/A and clean: the convention captured (raise-don't-catch on the MCP surface, stdout-is-sacred, named-logger-to-stderr, observability-floor + noqa-with-reason) governs error-handling style, not secret material. The documented logging substrate emits diagnostics to stderr only; nothing in scope writes secrets to logs. The `ERROR:`-prefixed stderr probe output (per the reconciler dep) is operational diagnostics, not sensitive data.
- (c) Least-privilege grants — N/A: no credential/role/grant is created or widened. Emptying the allowlist *tightens* enforcement (removes escape hatches), the opposite of an over-broad grant.
- (d) Secret lifecycle / no plaintext secrets — N/A: no secrets, IaC, or credentials are touched; no plaintext-secret surface is introduced.

T5c PASS as not-applicable — the application's actual (non-network, non-auth) domain has no security boundary in this chunk, and imposing a generic web-app security requirement here would be a false positive.
